---
title: "Session 13 · Logistic Diagnostics & Simpson's Paradox"
---

## Overview

A fitted logistic model is only as trustworthy as its diagnostics. In this session we ask how well a logistic regression actually fits, which observations exert undue influence, and how well it classifies --- reading deviance residuals, leverage, and the ROC curve much as we read residual plots in the linear half of the course. We then confront Simpson's Paradox, where adjusting for a single variable can *reverse* an association, a vivid reminder that which predictors we condition on is itself a modeling decision with substantive stakes.

## Learning objectives

By the end of this session, you will be able to:

- Assess logistic model fit using deviance residuals and goodness-of-fit summaries.
- Identify influential observations with leverage and Cook's-distance-style measures.
- Evaluate classification performance with a confusion matrix and the ROC curve's area under the curve.
- Explain how Simpson's Paradox arises and why a marginal and a conditional association can point in opposite directions.
- Decide which variables to adjust for, and justify that choice on substantive grounds.

## This session's materials

- {{< fa book-open-reader >}} **Read:** [Chapter 11: Logistic Regression Diagnostics](/notes/chapters/11-logistic-diagnostics.html){target="_blank"}
- {{< fa laptop-code >}} **Lab:** [Lab 11: Logistic Diagnostics](/labs/labs/lab11-logistic-diagnostics.html){target="_blank"}
- {{< fa pencil >}} **Homework due:** [Homework 8: Simpson's Paradox](/assignment/hw08.html)
- {{< fa circle-question >}} **Practice:** [Practice Quiz 8](/practice/chapters/quiz8-multiple-logistic-simpsons.html){target="_blank"}
- {{< fa circle-check >}} **Graded quiz:** [Module Quiz 8](/assignment/quizzes.html#quiz-8)

## Key concepts

| Concept | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| **Deviance residual** | A residual on the deviance scale used to flag poorly fit observations in a logistic model. |
| **Influence** | The degree to which a single observation changes the fitted model, measured by leverage and Cook's-distance-style statistics. |
| **Confusion matrix** | A cross-tabulation of predicted versus observed classes at a chosen probability cutoff. |
| **ROC curve** | A plot of the true-positive rate against the false-positive rate across all cutoffs. |
| **AUC** | The area under the ROC curve, summarizing a model's ability to discriminate the two classes. |
| **Simpson's Paradox** | A reversal in which an association seen in aggregate flips direction once a third variable is controlled. |
| **Marginal vs. conditional association** | The relationship between two variables ignoring, versus accounting for, a third. |
| **Adjustment** | Conditioning on a variable to estimate an effect free of its confounding influence. |
